brief:
The single most important signal this week is the absolute stillness across the entire yield curve — not a single basis point moved at any tenor — and that silence is deafening. When a curve refuses to reprice despite **9.13%** CPI, a **10%** repo rate, revenue collection limping at just **44.9%** of target, and a fiscal deficit that will inevitably demand heavier back-loaded borrowing in Q4 FY26, the market is telling us one of two things: either participants are so flush with liquidity that they can absorb anything at current levels, or they are frozen in uncertainty, waiting for a catalyst that hasn't arrived. I believe it is the former masking the latter. The T-bill complex cleared cleanly — the 91-day at **9.85%** drew a healthy bid-cover of **1.92x** with zero devolvement, and the 364-day at **10.07%** came in at **1.75x**, also clean. But step into the bond segment and the picture changes: the 10-year BGTB auction on March 8 managed only a **1.4x** bid-cover with **10% devolvement**, meaning Bangladesh Bank had to absorb unsold stock onto its own balance sheet. That is the crack in the façade. The front end is comfortable; the long end is not.

The structural story is one of massive excess liquidity — **৳1.9 lakh crore** sitting in the system — colliding with a curve that has become visibly distorted. Look at the shape carefully: the 2-year prints at **11.95%**, a full **80 basis points** above the 10-year at **11.15%** and **30 basis points** above the 15-year at **12.68%** only if you squint past the belly. More strikingly, the 2-year at **11.95%** is higher than the 5-year (**10.82%**), the 10-year (**11.15%**), and the 20-year (**11.65%**). This is not a normal curve; this is a market pricing acute near-term fiscal stress and rollover risk in the 1-to-3 year segment while the long end benefits from captive SLR demand. Banks are parking their SLR holdings in 10-year and 20-year paper for regulatory comfort, compressing those yields artificially, while the 2-year segment — too long for money-market desks, too short for SLR optimization — sits orphaned at punitive levels. The call money rate at **9.80%** hugging just below repo confirms that despite headline excess liquidity, the distribution is uneven; a handful of large banks are sitting on the surplus while mid-tier institutions are still accessing the corridor. The ADR at **75.8%** tells us private credit demand remains subdued, which is why that ৳1.9 lakh crore hasn't found its way into loans and instead presses down on government securities — but selectively, only where regulatory incentives exist.

The fiscal trajectory demands our full attention. With only **44.9%** of revenue collected and ADP implementation at a dismal **11.5%**, the government faces a classic Q4 squeeze: spending will accelerate sharply into June while revenues underperform, and the gap will be financed through treasury issuance. Interest costs already consume **22%** of revenue, and total debt at **৳21.5 lakh crore** — roughly **40%** of GDP — leaves limited room for missteps. Brent at **$78.4** and JKM LNG at **$12.8/mmbtu** are not helping the import bill, and with reserves at **$35.1 billion** and the taka at **121.2**, Bangladesh Bank has limited appetite to ease policy even if growth considerations might argue for it. Food inflation at **9.3%** ahead of Ramadan seasonality makes a rate cut in the next two months virtually impossible.

Here is the call: the 2-year BGTB at **11.95%** is the single best risk-adjusted opportunity on the curve right now. It sits **195 basis points** above the repo rate, **115 basis points** above the 10-year, and offers a carry that compensates handsomely for duration that is entirely manageable. If BB eventually eases — and the market will start pricing that possibility once CPI credibly breaks below 8.5% — the 2-year will reprice violently and favorably. Conversely, avoid adding 10-year duration at **11.15%** with a **1.4x** bid-cover and devolvement; that part of the curve is artificially compressed and vulnerable to a selloff when Q4 supply hits. Keep SLR portfolios in the 5-year at **10.82%** where bid-cover remains decent at **1.6x** and devolvement is zero. The catalyst to watch is the next BB monetary policy statement and any signal on the borrowing calendar revision — if the government announces incremental T-bill supply above ৳30,000 crore for Q4, the front end reprices higher and that 2-year window narrows fast.
